nativeRange.habitat: Generally found in fast-flowing rivers, streams, and estuarine reaches of West African coastal drainages, often associated with mangrove root masses, submerged crevices, and rocky substrates that provide both anchorage in current and daytime shelter.

decorPreferences: sand substrate (preferred over coarse gravel to protect fan appendages)
rockwork with crevices for daytime hiding
live plants such as java fern or anubias
perches positioned in the current for filter feeding

diet.notes: Obligate filter feeder that perches in a current and uses feather-like fan appendages to sieve fine suspended particles from the water column. Regular shrimp pellets and flakes are too coarse to be captured efficiently. Best fed with powdered or finely crushed foods, phytoplankton suspensions, baby brine shrimp, and detritus stirred up from the substrate. Squeezing a mature filter sponge into the tank also tends to release useful particulate.

avoidWith: Cichlids large enough to harass adult shrimp

summary: Large West African filter-feeding shrimp reaching around 9–15 cm, prized for its imposing build and color phases ranging from grey-blue to rusty red. Peaceful, shy, and best kept in groups in a well-aged, brisk-current freshwater tank.
careNotes:
The Vampire Shrimp is a large atyid filter-feeder native to fast-flowing West African rivers from Senegal to the Democratic Republic of the Congo, often inhabiting mangrove-influenced reaches. Earlier records placing the species in South America (Venezuela, Brazil) are generally now considered a case of mistaken identity with a related congener.

Coloration is famously variable and shifts with water chemistry, mood, and molt state — individuals can present as grey-white, light blue, rusty red-brown, or the deep blue "Blue Rhino" phase that harder water tends to bring out. The common names "African Fan Shrimp", "African Filter Shrimp", "Gabon Shrimp", "Giant African Filter Shrimp", "Blue Rhino Shrimp", and "Cameroon Shrimp" all refer to this single species.

They are obligate filter feeders: their fan-like claspers strain fine particles from the water column rather than picking food off the substrate. A moderate-to-strong current is therefore not optional but functional — a powerhead or strong return aimed across a perching rock helps deliver food. Sand or smooth fine substrate is preferred; sharp gravel can damage the delicate fans. Specimens are cryptic and tend to hide for most of the day, emerging at dusk or when food particulate is in the water.

Size reports vary widely: sources commonly cite around 7–10 cm in captivity, with wild-caught adults reaching 12–15 cm; the range here spans typical captive size to documented maximum. Lifespan in well-kept aquariums is generally reported at 3–5 years, with isolated accounts of considerably longer-lived individuals; the conservative 3–5 year range is used here.

The Shrimp Farm's care page (designated primary source) returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch, so its parameter values were taken from indexed search-engine snippets confirming temperature 70–80°F (~21–27°C), pH 6.8–7.5, dGH 6–20, dKH 2–15, TDS 175–300, and a 10–20 gallon recommendation. The Aquarium Co-Op site (designated secondary) does not host a dedicated care page for this species, so two alternative secondaries (Shrimp & Snail Breeder / Aquariumbreeder, and Aquariadise) were used to confirm the parameter envelope. Tank size is set at 75 L (~20 US gal) on the cautious-larger side of the source spread (range across sources: ~38–75 L) because adults are large, want sustained linear current, and benefit from group housing.

Vampire Shrimp are reported to be highly sensitive to copper and to dissolved heavy metals, so copper-based medications and untreated tap water with copper plumbing are best avoided. Like most decapods, they are also sensitive to ammonia and nitrite spikes; only mature, biologically established tanks are appropriate.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is effectively impossible in a standard freshwater setup. Females carry several hundred to several thousand small eggs for roughly 3–4 weeks, but the hatched larvae are amphidromous and require brackish to near-marine salinity (commonly cited around 28–30 g/L, i.e. specific gravity in the ~1.020 range) to survive and develop. Larval development takes approximately three to four months in saltwater before the post-larvae can be transitioned back to freshwater, a regime that has been achieved only in a handful of dedicated breeding setups and not reliably in the hobby. For practical purposes, breeding difficulty is "not in captivity."

primary.name: The Shrimp Farm
primary.notes: Designated primary source. Direct WebFetch returned HTTP 403; parameter values (temperature 70-80°F, pH 6.8-7.5, dGH 6-20, dKH 2-15, TDS 175-300, 10 gal minimum / 20 gal recommended) were taken from indexed search-engine snippets of this page and cross-verified against secondaries before use.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Designated secondary source per Stage A manifest but has no dedicated Vampire Shrimp care page; substituted two alternative secondaries (Shrimp & Snail Breeder and Aquariadise) per playbook section 3 fallback rule.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (Aquariumbreeder)
additional.notes: Substitute secondary. Confirmed adult size 12-15 cm (wild) / ~9-11 cm (captive), temperature 22-28°C, pH 6.8-7.2 core (6.0-8.0 tolerance), GH 6-20, KH 2-15, 15 gal / 70 L minimum, peaceful temperament, filter-feeder diet, brackish-water requirement for larval development (~28-30 g/L for ~4 months), and West African native range (Gabon / Cross River system / mangroves).

additional.name: Aquariadise
additional.notes: Substitute secondary. Confirmed adult size up to 15 cm, temperature 24-29°C, pH 6.5-7.5, KH 3-10, lifespan 3-5 years, 20 gal minimum, peaceful temperament, strong-flow requirement, sand substrate preference, and brackish/saltwater larval requirement.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts
additional.notes: Tertiary cross-check. Confirmed adult size 2-3 in typical / up to 6 in, temperature 75-84°F, pH 6.8-7.5, dGH 6-20, dKH 2-15, TDS 150-300, lifespan up to 5 years, 20 gal minimum, peaceful temperament, sensitivity to dissolved metals (supports copper-sensitive flag).

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Atyidae, order Decapoda) and confirmation that the species' true native range is West Africa from Senegal to the DRC, with historical South American records considered mistaken identity.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Molting is reported to be relatively infrequent compared to small dwarf shrimp, consistent with the species' slow metabolism and large adult size. Freshly molted individuals are particularly vulnerable and tend to retreat into shelter until the new exoskeleton hardens; the cast exuvia is typically left intact and is often consumed for calcium reclamation.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Larvae are amphidromous and require brackish to near-marine salinity (~28-30 g/L, roughly SG 1.020) for approximately 3-4 months of development before they can be transitioned to freshwater. The setup has been replicated in a handful of dedicated breeding projects but not reliably reproduced in the hobby, so for practical purposes this species is considered non-breedable in standard freshwater tanks.
